title: Logarithm calculator - compute log base b of any value
shortDescription: Calculate the logarithm of a value to any base.
description: A free online logarithm calculator that computes log base b of a value using log_b(x) = ln(x)/ln(b). Works with base 10, base 2, the natural log base e, or any custom base. Enter a positive value and a valid base to get instant, accurate results for math and science.
metaDescription: Free logarithm calculator to compute log base b of any positive value. Supports base 10, base 2, natural log (e), and custom bases with instant results.

result.label: Logarithm
result.empty: Enter a value and a base to see the logarithm.

sections.heading: What is a logarithm?
paragraphs: A logarithm answers the question: to what power must the base be raised to produce a given value? In other words, log_b(x) is the exponent that turns the base b into x.
Logarithms turn multiplication into addition and exponents into multiplication, which makes them essential in science, engineering, finance, and computing.

sections.heading: How the calculation works
paragraphs: This tool uses the change-of-base formula log_b(x) = ln(x)/ln(b), so it can handle any valid base. The value must be greater than 0, and the base must be greater than 0 and not equal to 1.
Common bases include 10 (common log), 2 (binary log), and e (natural log, about 2.71828).

howTo.name: Enter the value
howTo.text: Type the positive value you want to take the logarithm of into the Value field.

howTo.name: Enter the base
howTo.text: Enter the base, such as 10, 2, or e. It must be greater than 0 and not equal to 1.

howTo.name: Read the result
howTo.text: The calculator instantly shows log base b of your value.

faq.q: What base does the calculator use by default?
faq.a: You choose the base. Common choices are 10, 2, and e, but any base greater than 0 and not equal to 1 works.

faq.q: Why must the value be positive?
faq.a: Logarithms are only defined for values greater than 0. The logarithm of zero or a negative number does not exist in real numbers.
A base of 1 raised to any power is always 1, so it cannot produce other values, making the logarithm undefined.

faq.q: How do I compute a natural log?
faq.a: Set the base to e (about 2.71828) to get the natural logarithm, ln(x).
Yes. Any positive base other than 1 is allowed, including decimals like 1.5 or 2.5.
